name: Crafting Queue
description: Crafting jobs are placed into ordered slots and complete after station, recipe, and progress rules are satisfied.
design_notes: A crafting queue turns crafting into scheduled production. It needs queue_slot_index ownership, craft_started_at_ms, station availability, cancellation policy, offline progress rules, and duplicate completion protection so jobs cannot be lost, accelerated accidentally, or claimed more than once.
design_purpose: Let long-running crafting happen without forcing the player to wait inside a menu.
Create capacity and prioritization decisions around station slots, batch sizes, and materials.
Support survival and base loops where production continues while the player explores or manages threats.
player_fantasy: The player feels like they are managing a workshop that keeps producing planned items over time.

parameters.name: crafting_queue_id
parameters.description: Stable queue identifier used for station ownership, persistence, multiplayer access, and UI selection.

parameters.name: queue_slot_index
parameters.description: Ordered slot that controls processing order, cancellation behavior, and drag-reorder operations.

parameters.name: craft_started_at_ms
parameters.description: Authority timestamp captured when the queue job begins active processing.

parameters.name: crafting_work_ms
parameters.description: Required processing window for the job after recipe, station, and queue-slot validation.

parameters.name: batch_size
parameters.description: Number of recipe executions represented by the queued job and completed output grant.

parameters.name: cancellation_refund_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule used when the player cancels a queued job before completion.

edge_cases: A station is destroyed, moved, or loses fuel while it has active and pending queue slots.
The player cancels a job on the same tick that completion is being claimed.
Offline progress completes several jobs while the output inventory is full.
Two players reorder the same station queue from different clients.
common_bugs: Completed jobs grant output every login because completion_claimed is not persisted.
Cancellation refunds ingredients after the output has already been produced by the server.
Queue sorting changes queue_slot_index without updating UI selection, causing cancellation of the wrong job.
Offline progress uses local device clock and can be exploited by changing system time.
balancing_notes: Queue length controls production scale; too many slots can remove meaningful station and storage decisions.
Long jobs work best when players can plan, cancel, or reprioritize without losing opaque amounts of progress.
Offline progress should respect game economy pacing and may need caps, fuel checks, or server timestamps.
accessibility_notes: Show job order, remaining progress, cancellation outcome, and blocked output state in readable text.
Support keyboard and controller queue reordering without requiring precise drag gestures.

implementation_notes.unity: Represent each queue entry as data with crafting_queue_id, queue_slot_index, recipe_id, batch_size, craft_started_at_ms, and completion_claimed. The scheduler should compute progress from an authority clock, then claim output through the same inventory transaction used by recipe crafting.
implementation_notes.godot: Keep queue state in a Resource or autoload service rather than in the crafting UI scene. Save queue entries with station id, slot index, start tick, and claim state; Control nodes should render projected progress without mutating the queue.
implementation_notes.unreal: A station Actor Component can own replicated queue entries while the server validates reorder, cancel, and claim requests. Use server timestamps for offline or asynchronous progress, and replicate completion events separately from cosmetic UI notifications.
implementation_notes.web: Store queue entries as plain records and process them through a deterministic scheduler. Browser clients should not trust local wall-clock completion for authoritative saves; use server timestamps or capped simulated offline progress.

relationships.reason: Crafting queues depend on recipe validation, ingredient reservation, and output grant transactions.
Queued production reserves or spends material stacks and must refund them on valid cancellation.
Workbench stations can provide queue slots, station tier requirements, and completion access rules.

example_games.title: Factorio
example_games.note: Uses production queues and machines to process recipes over time.

example_games.title: Don't Starve
example_games.note: Uses timed crafting and station-driven survival production.
